wineries.name: Brave & Maiden Estate
wineries.excerpt: First visit but certainly not the last!
wineries.review:
Ring the bell, wait for the gates to open, and just like that, you're in a different world. The views hit you immediately. To your left, a stunning tasting room. Off in the distance, a château-like estate that looks like it belongs in the French countryside. Pristine, dramatic, and completely unexpected.


We were greeted warmly the moment we walked up, no awkward waiting around, just a genuine welcome and the beginning of what turned into one of our favorite tastings in a long time. We got a full tour of the facility before settling into a private library room tasting. Inside and out, the place feels like a wine spa. Gorgeous design, relaxed energy, zero pretension.

We started with the 2023 Limited Sauvignon Blanc, crisp, bright, and exactly what you want to open with on a warm afternoon. Then came the reds. The Rule of Thirds — their Rhône-style blend of Syrah, Grenache and Mourvèdre — was layered and smooth. The Limited Merlot followed, and then the Bequest, their Bordeaux flagship built around Cabernet Sauvignon. That one honestly stopped us mid-conversation. Our host even pulled out a few bottles that weren't on the tasting menu, which is always a sign you're in the right hands.
We paired everything with a charcuterie board — worth every penny, especially on a full day of tasting. Perfect pacing for a long afternoon of pouring.

## Who Should Go

Anyone who wants a wine experience that actually feels like an experience. Don't rush this one  clear your afternoon, enjoy every pour, and let it unfold.

##The Kazmi Take

**Boutique, elevated, and worth every penny. Brave & Maiden just locked down a permanent spot in our Santa Barbara rotation. Can't wait to crack those magnums.**

wineries.name: Crown Point Vineyard
wineries.review:
Let's settle something right away. Santa Barbara makes Cabernet Sauvignon. Great Cabernet Sauvignon. The kind that holds its own against its neighbors to the north in Napa, without the premium price tag of those AVAs and, in my experience, with a far more personal touch. Happy Canyon is where this is happening and Crown Point is the crown jewel of the conversation.
I stumbled upon them about four years ago. Santa Barbara has been part of our rotation for a long time and discovering new vineyards has always been part of the fun. How do you keep it fresh? How do you keep it new? I knew Happy Canyon was making some serious Cabs but it felt like a recent phenomenon. Crown Point was founded in 2015 so when I found them I knew we were getting in early on something special.
Reservation only. Rachel and I showed up and the property does what all the great ones do, it stops you before you even get out of the car. You pull through the gate, drive a slow winding road through the estate, horse stables to your left, landscape so picturesque you start wondering if it is real. By the time you pull in and park, Tiffany, our favorite host, is already there waiting. Her smile is the kind that feels almost like a hug. The kind that tells you immediately you are in the right place.
The tour of the estate begins and it is something else entirely. The main house is fit for Louis XIV. A twelve person dining room that makes you want to call everyone you love and plan a dinner immediately. A small ballroom. Limited production. Everything intentional and elegant. Then you walk out to the vast green space and the valley opens up in front of you, panoramic, breathtaking, the owner's home sitting quietly in the distance. Rachel and I stopped in our tracks. You ask yourself the same thing every time. Is this heaven?
On our last visit Tiffany took us somewhere we had never been in six previous visits. A gator ride up to the actual point of the property, just the two of us, Tiffany, and the two dogs trotting alongside. Behind the scenes in a way I had never experienced there before. That is how personal Crown Point makes it when they know you. That kind of hospitality is rare anywhere, let alone at a winery.
They are now making a sparkling wine in the Champagne style, sourced from Champagne itself. Beautiful, fresh, and the perfect way to begin the tasting. We settle in and then the real magic happens. The reserve tasting is the way to go, three spectacular Cabernet blends, predominantly Cab, ripe and lush and velvety. They are young wines so they are ready to drink now but they are also built to evolve. Lay them down for a few years and they will reward you. Drink them within the first ten and they will not disappoint. The service is perfectly paced, they tell you everything you want to know about the wines and then step back and let you be.
Rachel and I sit there every time, glasses in hand, valley stretching out in front of us, and we ask each other the same question. What are we doing in Dallas? It has become a running joke at this point, one we have no real interest in resolving.
Every year I bring a group of people I work with, friends with benefits as I like to call them. Crown Point is the one stop we have never missed. It has converted people who have never bought a bottle of wine in their life into walking out with multiple bottles every single time. That is the power of this place. It is not just a tasting. It is an experience that changes something in you.

## Who Should Go
If you love Cabernet Sauvignon this is a non-negotiable stop on your Santa Barbara trip. But honestly, even if you are not a Cab person, Crown Point belongs on your list. This is for anyone who wants a wine experience that feels genuinely personal and unhurried. Couples, small groups, wine lovers, and especially people who think they are not wine people yet. Reserve your tasting ahead of time, it is reservation only, and do yourself a favor and opt for the reserve tasting. It is worth every penny.

## The Kazmi Take
**Six visits and it gets better every time. Crown Point is the ultimate showstopper in Santa Barbara County. If you are not leaving with at least a bottle or two you were not paying attention. Paramount for Rachel and I, and it will be for you too. 🍷**

wineries.name: Château La Nerthe, Châteauneuf-du-Pape, Southern Rhône
wineries.review:
In 2024 Rachel and I embarked on what I can only describe as our most epic wine trip ever. Provence to Burgundy. A trip we had been dreaming about for years. There were many highlights along the way but for Rachel there was always going to be one non-negotiable destination. Châteauneuf-du-Pape. It was on her list and nothing was going to change that.
Our guide Thierry was exceptional. Accommodating, knowledgeable, and as we quickly discovered, playful in the best possible way. When he figured out that Rachel and I knew a little more about wine than he initially expected, something shifted. He started looking at us differently. And then he started setting things up. With a wink.
The final grand finish he had planned for us was Château La Nerthe. Driving up to the property you immediately understand why this place is considered one of the Meccas of the appellation. Stone covered vines stretch out across the land as far as you can see. The famous galets, those ancient rounded stones that absorb heat during the day and release it at night, line the vineyard floors as if a river had been there centuries ago and left them behind as a gift. It is breathtaking before you even step inside.
As we walked in Thierry announced to our hosts that we really knew wine. I was in the background quietly gesturing for him to stop. The last thing I wanted was to be put on the spot in front of people who have dedicated their entire lives to this appellation. But it was too late.
Our host decided to take me up on my knowledge and before I knew it I was blind tasting through Château La Nerthe.
First came a white. No label on the bottle. I held it up, studied the color. Caramel. Honey. Dark hay. I put my nose in. Similar notes came back. Caramel, honey, a touch of sweetness. I could tell immediately it had serious age on it. Thierry stood there watching me, waiting. I took a breath and shot out 2009. Wrong. It was a 2001. Twenty three years old and absolutely magical. Still so much life left in that bottle it was almost unfair. I turned around and Rachel was behind me clapping like I had just done something remarkable, which honestly felt like I had. Before I knew what was happening I was convincing our host to bottle it, label it, and ship three bottles back to Dallas. I still have two of them. I am in no rush.
Then came the reds. He set a Châteauneuf-du-Pape in front of me and asked me about the grape profile. Châteauneuf-du-Pape can have up to eighteen permitted varieties. I was not going to back down. I held the glass over white paper. One of the clearest examples of the appellation I had ever seen. Immediately on the nose I got red cherry, a little red plum. I swirled it, sipped it, and everything in me wanted to just say Grenache. So I did. I called it a Grenache dominant Châteauneuf-du-Pape.
He smiled. It was a trick question. It was their only 100% Grenache bottling. I was wrong about the blend but right about the grape. He was impressed I caught it. Honestly so was I.
The hospitality throughout all of this was extraordinary. The testing was playful, never pretentious, and done with genuine warmth. We tasted through some of the most remarkable bottles of the trip that afternoon, took home nearly two cases of wine, and have been slowly working through them since. We opened one of the older vintages not long after getting home and it drank as fantastically as anything we had that day. So much life left.
Do not sleep on Château La Nerthe. The good news is you do not have to travel to France to find it. I have seen it at restaurants and wine shops right here in the States. Seek it out.

## Who Should Go
If you ever find yourself in the Southern Rhône this is a non-negotiable visit. Châteauneuf-du-Pape is already one of the great wine destinations in the world and Château La Nerthe is the peak of that experience. Go with an open mind, engage your hosts, and do not be afraid to show what you know. The experience only gets richer when you do. For those who cannot make the trip, find their wines at a local shop or restaurant and start there. Either way you need this producer in your life.

## The Kazmi Take
**One of the greatest wine experiences of our lives. The blind tastings, the 2001 white that stopped us cold, the 100% Grenache that fooled me just enough, and the hospitality that made all of it feel like an afternoon with old friends. Château La Nerthe is the peak expression of Châteauneuf-du-Pape. Full stop. 🍷**
